function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Connectivity revenue includes both consumer broadband and enterprise/government applications. Based on disclosed metrics and definitions, what revenue streams can be reasonably inferred within Connectivity and which remain unquantified? What modeling limitations follow from this disclosure structure?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's Connectivity revenue segment - what revenue streams are included, which can be quantified, and what modeling limitations exist. I need to examine the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the Connectivity revenue disclosure structure.

Let me first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to examine the revenue breakdown and connectivity segment details.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Good, now let me search for information about Connectivity revenue segments and breakdowns.

arguments.prompt: What are the revenue segments and sub-segments described in {{spacex_s1}}? Specifically, what does the Connectivity segment include? What are the different revenue streams within Connectivity such as consumer broadband and enterprise/government applications? Look for any revenue definitions, breakdowns, or descriptions of Connectivity revenue.

What financial metrics and revenue figures are disclosed for the Connectivity segment in {{spacex_s1}}? Look for any tables showing Connectivity revenue, subscriber counts, ARPU, Starlink metrics, broadband revenue, enterprise revenue, government revenue, or any quantitative data related to the Connectivity business.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is a clear breakdown of the **Connectivity segment**, its revenue streams, and how they are defined. ### **Connectivity Segment Overview** The Connectivity segment operates **Starlink**, the company's global high-speed, low-latency broadband and mobile connectivity service. It delivers services to consumers, enterprises, and governments via its constellation of Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites. --- ### **Revenue Streams & Sub-Segments** Revenue within the Connectivity segment is generated from three primary categories: #### 1. **Consumer Revenue** * **Description:** Subscription fees from individual consumers for Starlink broadband service. * **Sub-segments & Details:** * **Consumer Broadband:** The core offering providing home internet with "fiber-like download speeds." Revenue is driven by the number of **Starlink Subscribers** and the **Average Revenue Per User (ARPU)**. * **Revenue Recognition:** Recognized ratably over the monthly subscription term. Revenue is typically composed of: * A recurring monthly subscription fee based on geographic market and download speed. * A one-time sale of the **Starlink Kit** (terminal and related equipment). * **Scale:** Consumer subscribers represented **over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue** in 2024. #### 2. **Enterprise & Government Revenue** * **Description:** Revenue from commercial businesses and public-sector entities. * **Sub-segments & Details:** * **Enterprise Connectivity:** Services for businesses, including: * **Aviation** * **Maritime** * **Mobility** * Other enterprise solutions (e.g., remote site connectivity). * **Government Connectivity (Starshield):** Long-term, secure satellite network contracts for government and national security applications. Revenue is often recognized over time using a **percentage-of-completion** method. * **Starlink Mobile:** This offering is explicitly included in this category. It provides mobile connectivity services (initially SMS and OTT voice) through partnerships with Mobile Network Operators (MNOs). Revenue is shared with MNO partners based on services included in their customer plans. * **Revenue Recognition:** Contracts are often structured as a combination of subscriptions, data consumption, and capacity, or on a percentage-of-completion basis. #### 3. **Revenue from MNO Partners (Starlink Mobile)** * **Description:** While part of Enterprise & Government, this stream has a distinct model. The company charges MNOs either a **fixed fee** or a **per-mobile user fee**, which is typically passed through to the end customer via the carrier. --- ### **Key Financial Drivers & Metrics** * **Growth Driver:** Subscriber growth and increased adoption by enterprise and government customers. * **Offsetting Factor:** **ARPU decline**, primarily due to international expansion and the addition of lower-priced service plans. * **Financial Reporting:** * The segment's profitability is measured by **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**. * **Deferred Revenue** primarily relates to enterprise and government contracts within this segment. ### **Summary Table of Connectivity Revenue Streams** | Revenue Stream | Description | Key Components & Recognition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Consumer** | Subscriptions from individual users for Starlink broadband. | - Recurring monthly fee (recognized ratably).
- One-time Starlink Kit sale (recognized on delivery/installation). | | **Enterprise & Government** | Contracts with businesses and public entities. | - **Enterprise:** Aviation, Maritime, Mobility, etc.
- **Government (Starshield):** Long-term secure contracts.
- Often uses % of completion or subscription/data models. | | **Starlink Mobile** | Mobile connectivity via MNO partnerships. | - Revenue-sharing with ~30 MNO partners.
- Included in the **Enterprise & Government** category in financial tables. | In essence, the Connectivity segment's revenue is built on a consumer subscription base, amplified by large-scale contracts with enterprises and governments, and further extended through mobile carrier partnerships.

Based on the provided financial documents, here is a comprehensive breakdown of the financial metrics and revenue figures disclosed for the **Connectivity segment**:

---

### **1. Annual Revenue & Segment Income**
| **Metric** | **2025** | **2024** | **2023** |
|------------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Total Connectivity Segment Revenue** | $11,387 million | $7,599 million | $3,869 million |
| Year-over-Year Growth | 49.8% | 96.4% | — |
| **Income (Loss) from Operations** | $4,423 million | $2,006 million | $469 million |
| Year-over-Year Growth | 120.4% | 327.9% | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | $7,168 million | $3,849 million | $1,602 million |
| Year-over-Year Growth | 86.2% | 140.3% | — |

---

### **2. Quarterly Revenue & Profitability (Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025)**
| **Metric** | **Q1 2026** | **Q1 2025** | **Change** |
|------------|-------------|-------------|------------|
| **Total Connectivity Segment Revenue** | $3,257 million | $2,475 million | +31.6% |
| **Income from Operations** | $1,188 million | $1,033 million | +15.0% |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | $2,087 million | $1,618 million | +29.0% |

---

### **3. Revenue Disaggregation**
| **Revenue Type** | **2025** | **2024** | **2023** | **Q1 2026** | **Q1 2025** |
|------------------|----------|----------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Consumer** | $7,208 million | $4,830 million | $2,817 million | $2,148 million | $1,492 million |
| **Enterprise & Government** | $4,179 million | $2,769 million | $1,052 million | $1,109 million | $983 million |
| **Products (Starlink Kit)** | Included in Connectivity | Included in Connectivity | Included in Connectivity | Included in Connectivity | Included in Connectivity |

---

### **4. Key Operational Metrics (Starlink)**
| **Metric** | **Q1 2026** | **Q1 2025** | **2025** | **2024** | **2023** |
|------------|-------------|-------------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Starlink Subscribers (millions)** | 10.3 | 5.0 | 8.9 | 4.4 | 2.3 |
| **Starlink ARPU ($/month)** | $66 | $86 | $81 | $91 | $99 |

---

### **5. Cost & Expense Breakdown (2025)**
| **Expense Category** | **2025** | **2024** | **2023** |
|----------------------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Cost of Revenue** | $5,921 million | $4,768 million | $2,786 million |
| **Research & Development** | $575 million | $453 million | $381 million |
| **SG&A** | $468 million | $333 million | $233 million |
| **Depreciation & Amortization** | $2,376 million | $1,508 million | $884 million |
| **Share-Based Compensation** | $369 million | $296 million | $249 million |
| **Capital Expenditures** | $4,178 million | $3,498 million | $2,455 million |

---

### **6. Q1 2026 Expense Drivers**
- **Cost of Revenue** increased by $437 million (+36.0%) due to:
  - Higher depreciation ($276 million) from satellite launches.
  - Increased ground operations, installation, and warranty costs.
- **R&D** increased by $82 million (+66.7%) for next-gen satellites and ground equipment.
- **SG&A** increased to support international expansion.

---

### **7. Growth Drivers (MD&A Commentary)**
- **Consumer Revenue**: Driven by **104.7% subscriber growth** in Q1 2026, partially offset by a **22.9% decline in ARPU** due to international expansion and lower-priced plans.
- **Enterprise & Government Revenue**: Growth in **aviation, maritime, mobility, and government connectivity** (e.g., Starshield contracts).
- **Profitability**: Scale efficiencies from LEO satellite constellations and verticalized supply chain.

---

### **Summary**
The **Connectivity segment** (primarily Starlink) shows:
1. **Explosive revenue growth** (96% in 2024, 50% in 2025, 32% Q1 2026).
2. **Rapid subscriber expansion** (from 2.3M in 2023 to 10.3M in Q1 2026).
3. **Strong profitability** (income from operations grew from $469M in 2023 to $4.4B in 2025).
4. **Trade-off of ARPU for scale** in international markets.
5. **High capital investment** in satellite infrastructure and R&D.

All figures are sourced from SpaceX’s unaudited financial disclosures in the provided MD&A and segment reporting tables.

2. Whether Starlink Kit revenue is separately quantified from subscription revenue

4. ARPU breakdown by customer type
5. Any other quantification gaps

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what specific revenue sub-categories are disclosed within Connectivity? Is Starlink Kit/hardware revenue separated from subscription revenue? Is Starshield revenue separately quantified? Is Starlink Mobile revenue broken out? Are there any disclosures about the mix between recurring subscription revenue and one-time equipment revenue within Connectivity?

In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the filing say about the definition and composition of ARPU for Starlink? Is ARPU disclosed separately for consumer vs enterprise vs government customers? What are the limitations of the ARPU metric as described? Are subscriber counts broken out by customer type or geography?

In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the filing disclose about Starshield government connectivity revenue? Is Starshield revenue separately quantified within the Enterprise & Government category? What is the revenue recognition method for Starshield contracts? Are there any backlog or contract value disclosures for Starshield?

results.content:
Based on the provided MD&A excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of the revenue disclosures for the **Connectivity segment**:

### **1. Primary Disaggregation of Connectivity Revenue**
The company breaks down Connectivity revenue into two main categories:
*   **Consumer**
*   **Enterprise & Government**

This is explicitly shown in the revenue disaggregation tables and footnotes. For example:
| | Year Ended December 31, 2025 |
| :--- | :--- |
| Consumer | $7,208 |
| Enterprise & Government (1) | $4,179 |
| **Connectivity** | **$11,387** |
*(1) Enterprise & Government revenue includes revenue from Starlink Mobile service offerings.*

### **2. Specific Answers to Your Questions**

*   **Is Starlink Kit/hardware revenue separated from subscription revenue?**
    *   **Not separately quantified in the reported segment figures.** The text explains the *accounting treatment* (Kit revenue recognized at point of delivery vs. service revenue recognized over time), but the disaggregated revenue tables (Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government) **do not** show a further split between "products" (kits) and "services" (subscriptions). The company's statement that "All of products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment" implies hardware sales are included within the Consumer and Enterprise & Government lines, but the exact amount is not disclosed.

*   **Is Starshield revenue separately quantified?**
    *   **No.** Starshield is discussed as a product offering for government customers and is a component of the **"Enterprise & Government"** revenue category. There is no separate line item or dollar amount provided for Starshield revenue alone.

*   **Is Starlink Mobile revenue broken out?**
    *   **Not separately quantified.** The footnote clearly states that "Enterprise & Government revenue includes revenue from Starlink Mobile service offerings." Therefore, Starlink Mobile revenue is included within the **$4,179 million** "Enterprise & Government" total for 2025, but its specific contribution is not disclosed.

*   **Are there any disclosures about the mix between recurring subscription revenue and one-time equipment revenue?**
    *   **Qualitative, but not quantitative.** The text thoroughly explains the revenue recognition policies: services are recognized over time (ratably or based on usage), while hardware (Starlink Kits) is recognized upon delivery/installation. However, the financial tables **do not** provide a numerical split (e.g., $X million from subscriptions vs. $Y million from kit sales). The mix is only implied through the growth drivers discussed (e.g., "Starlink Subscriber base continued to grow" driving consumer revenue).

### **Summary**
The Connectivity segment's revenue is disclosed at a **two-tier level**:
1.  **Primary Level:** Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government.
2.  **Underlying Components:** The disclosures explain that within these categories, revenue includes both **recurring subscription fees** (broadband, mobile, Starshield services) and **one-time hardware sales** (Starlink Kits), with Starlink Mobile and Starshield included in the Enterprise & Government line.

However, the company **does not** provide a granular, quantitative breakdown of these underlying components (e.g., separating kit revenue from subscription revenue, or separately stating Starshield or Starlink Mobile sales). The analysis is focused on the drivers of growth within the disclosed categories (subscriber count, ARPU, enterprise vs. government).

Based on the provided S-1 excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of the Starlink ARPU metric, its composition, limitations, and subscriber segmentation.

### 1. Definition and Composition of Starlink Subscriber ARPU

The filing provides a precise, technical definition:

*   **Calculation:** "We calculate ARPU as service revenue generated from **Starlink Subscribers** during the period divided by (i) the average number of Starlink Subscribers during the period and by (ii) the number of months in the period."
*   **Composition:** ARPU is **exclusively based on revenue from "Starlink Subscribers."**
*   **Scope of "Starlink Subscribers":** This is defined as unique Service Lines assigned to **consumer accounts** (e.g., Residential, Roam) and **business accounts** (e.g., Local Priority, Global Priority) that are **directly managed through Starlink.com**. It explicitly **does not include** "managed enterprise and government customers with contracts in domains including aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites and government entities."

**Conclusion:** ARPU is **not disclosed separately** for consumer vs. enterprise vs. government customers because it is, by definition, a metric that **excludes enterprise and government contract revenue** from its numerator. It only captures revenue from the self-service consumer and business segments.

### 2. Strategy and the Planned Decline of ARPU

The filing states that SpaceX's strategy is **not focused on increasing ARPU**. Instead:
*   The goal is "driving sustainable revenue growth and expanding our margins through operational efficiencies and technological advancements."
*   They "generally expect Starlink Subscriber ARPU to continue to decline" due to three factors:
    1.  Growth in subscribers outside North America (which have lower purchasing power).
    2.  Addition of lower-priced service plans.
    3.  Adjustments to monthly fees for broadband offerings.
*   They expect this ARPU decline to be "offset by increased scale and technological advancement," leading to overall revenue growth and cost reduction.

**Example:** The reported monthly ARPU dropped from **$91 (2024) to $81 (2025) to $66 (Q1 2026)**, aligning with their stated strategy.

### 3. Limitations of the ARPU Metric (As Described in the Filing)

The filing itself highlights key limitations of ARPU as a performance indicator:

*   **Not Comprehensive for Total Business:** ARPU is a narrow metric that excludes high-value, contract-based revenue from major enterprise (aviation, maritime) and government (Starshield) customers. Using it in isolation would significantly understate the financial performance and total revenue of the Connectivity segment.
*   **Not a Measure of Profitability:** A declining ARPU is presented as intentional. The metric does not reflect economies of scale, technological improvements in satellite and launch costs, or overall segment profitability, which showed strong growth (e.g., Segment Income from Operations up from $469M in 2023 to $4.4B in 2025).
*   **Metric is Intentionally Temporary:** Its decline is a strategic outcome, not necessarily a sign of weakness. The filing uses it to demonstrate penetration into less affluent markets and broader accessibility, which is core to their long-term vision of "expanding global connectivity."

### 4. Subscriber Count Breakout

*   **By Customer Type (Consumer vs. Enterprise/Government):** **No.** The key metric "Starlink Subscribers" is reported as a single aggregate number (~10.3 million as of Q1 2026). It is broken down only at a high level between "Personal" and "Business" subscription plans *within* the self-service category, but not between self-service and managed enterprise/government contracts.
*   **By Geography:** **No, not in the provided excerpts.** The filing discusses global expansion and uses region-specific ARPU assumptions for TAM calculations, but it does not break out the reported subscriber count by geography.

**Summary Table:**

| Aspect | What the Filing Says |
| :--- | :--- |
| **ARPU Definition** | Service revenue from **Starlink Subscribers** (consumer/self-service business) divided by average subscribers and months. |
| **ARPU Composition** | **Only includes** consumer & self-service business plan revenue. **Excludes** all managed enterprise/government contract revenue. |
| **Strategic Intent** | ARPU is expected to **decline** as a result of focusing on scale and global accessibility. Growth is expected from volume and cost efficiencies. |
| **Reported Trend** | Clearly declining: $91/mo (2024) -> $81/mo (2025) -> $66/mo (Q1 2026). |
| **Key Limitations** | 1) Excludes major revenue streams (enterprise/gov). 2) Decline is intentional, not necessarily negative. 3) Does not reflect overall profitability. |
| **Subscriber Breakout** | Aggregate number only (~10.3M). **No split** provided between self-service and managed enterprise/government customers, nor by geography. |

Based on the provided financial filing excerpts, here is a detailed disclosure on Starshield government connectivity revenue:

### 1. **Segment Placement**
Starshield revenue is **not** reported as a separate line item. It is included within the **"Enterprise & Government"** sub-category of the **Connectivity segment**. This is clarified in the revenue disaggregation tables, where a footnote states:
> *(1) Enterprise & Government revenue includes revenue from Starlink Mobile service offerings.*
While Starlink Mobile is explicitly mentioned, the context confirms Starshield (a government-focused secure network) also falls under this category.

### 2. **Revenue Recognition Method**
Starshield contracts are recognized using the **cost-to-cost input method**. This method is applied to long-term contracts because:
*   The Company's performance creates an asset with no alternative use and has an enforceable right to payment for performance to date.
*   Revenue is recorded based on costs incurred to date relative to the **total estimated cost at completion**.
*   This requires **significant management judgment** to estimate total costs, including assumptions on labor hours, shared cost allocation, satellite material costs, and expected technological changes.
*   Changes in estimates are recognized on a cumulative catch-up basis.

### 3. **Revenue Quantification**
The filings do **not** separately quantify Starshield revenue within the Enterprise & Government line. It is aggregated with enterprise connectivity and Starlink Mobile revenue. However, some key performance context is provided:
*   For 2024, Connectivity segment revenue from enterprise and government customers increased by **$1,717 million**, which was "primarily driven by... growth in our government connectivity business of **$1,250 million**." This government connectivity growth would include Starshield.
*   In the 2025 MD&A, the increase in Enterprise & Government revenue is attributed to growth in both enterprise connectivity and government connectivity.

### 4. **Backlog and Contract Value Disclosures**
Starshield contributes to the company's total **backlog**. Key disclosures include:
*   **Total Backlog:** As of March 31, 2026, backlog was **$27,621 million**, of which **$13,236 million** was recognized as deferred revenue.
*   **Recognition Timeline:** Approximately **36%** of the total backlog is expected to be recognized within one year, **46%** between one and three years, and the remaining **18%** thereafter.
*   **Nature of Backlog:** The backlog consists of performance obligations for which work remains to be performed under enforceable agreements. This includes Starshield contracts, which are long-term and complex.

### 5. **Critical Audit Matter (CAM)**
The auditor explicitly identified the **"Estimate of Total Cost at Completion for Certain Contracts Recognized Over Time"** (which includes Starshield contracts) as a **Critical Audit Matter**. This underscores the materiality of these contracts and the significance of the management judgments involved in their revenue recognition.

### Summary
In short, **Starshield government revenue is a significant and growing component of the Enterprise & Government sub-segment within Connectivity, recognized over time using a cost-to-cost method that involves substantial estimation.** Its specific revenue figure is not disclosed separately, but its financial impact is reflected in the growth of government connectivity revenue and is embedded in the company's substantial backlog of future contract work.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a very comprehensive picture. Let me also check if there's any disclosure about the Starlink Kit revenue specifically, and if there's any way to back into hardware vs. subscription revenue.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, is there any disclosure about the amount of products revenue or hardware revenue within the Connectivity segment? Look for any mention of Starlink Kit revenue, terminal sales, or equipment revenue that might be separately quantified or that could help estimate the split between hardware and subscription revenue. Also look for any discussion of deferred revenue composition within Connectivity.

In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the filing say about Starlink Mobile partnerships with MNOs? How many MNO partners are there? What is the revenue model - is it revenue sharing, fixed fee, per-user fee? Is there any quantification of Starlink Mobile revenue or number of mobile users connected?

results.content:
Based on the extensive excerpts you provided, here is a detailed analysis of what is disclosed—and what is not—regarding **Starlink Kit (hardware) revenue** and **deferred revenue composition** within the Connectivity segment.

### 1. Is there a separate disclosure of products/hardware revenue (e.g., Starlink Kit sales) within the Connectivity segment?

**No, there is no explicit, standalone disclosure of the amount of "products revenue" or "hardware revenue" for the Connectivity segment in the consolidated financial statements or segment results.**

Here’s what the documents *do* tell us:

*   **Revenue Recognition Policy:** The accounting note clearly states that contracts often contain **two distinct performance obligations**:
    1.  The broadband subscription service (recognized over time).
    2.  The sale of the **Starlink Kit** (recognized at a point in time, typically upon delivery).
    This confirms that hardware and service revenue are both components of Connectivity segment revenue.

*   **Consolidated Presentation:** The MD&A and financial tables show **Connectivity segment revenue as a single, combined figure** (e.g., $7,799 million for 2025 vs. $4,011 million for 2024). It is not broken down further into "hardware" and "subscription" line items in the provided excerpts.

*   **Drivers Mentioned:** The performance discussion focuses on **aggregate revenue growth**, attributed to increases in both:
    *   **Consumer subscriber numbers** (linked to service revenue).
    *   **Enterprise and government sales** (which can include kits and service contracts).
    *   **Mobile connectivity business growth.**

*   **Cost of Revenue Components:** The note on cost of revenue within the Connectivity segment **lists "Starlink Kit costs"** as a specific expense item. This indirectly confirms that kit sales are a meaningful cost driver, but the associated revenue remains embedded within the total segment revenue.

**Conclusion on Hardware Revenue:** While the financial statements clearly account for Starlink Kit sales as a separate performance obligation, the **quantitative split between service revenue and hardware revenue for the Connectivity segment is not disclosed** in the provided financial statements, notes, or MD&A excerpts. An analyst would need to estimate this based on shipment volumes, average selling prices, and other non-public data.

### 2. Is there any discussion of deferred revenue composition within the Connectivity segment?

**Yes, there is a partial discussion, but it does not provide a specific balance for Connectivity deferred revenue.**

The relevant disclosure is found in the revenue recognition note and related sections:

*   **General Policy:** "Deferred revenue is recorded when cash payments are received or due, in advance of the Company’s performance."
*   **Primary Drivers:** **"Deferred revenue primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts."**
*   **Company-Wide Balances:** The notes provide **consolidated deferred revenue balances**:
    *   **$10,179 million** as of December 31, 2024.
    *   **$12,116 million** as of December 31, 2025.
    *   It also notes that $4,080 million of the 2024 balance was recognized as revenue in 2025.

**What this tells us about Connectivity Deferred Revenue:**
1.  **Enterprise & Government Focus:** The composition disclosure explicitly states that a primary source of deferred revenue is **Connectivity enterprise and government contracts**. This makes sense, as these contracts (like Starshield) are likely longer-term and may involve upfront payments or milestone billing.
2.  **Consumer Deferred Revenue:** The policy for consumer broadband services ("month-to-month" contracts where revenue recognized equals amount billed) suggests **minimal deferred revenue from standard consumer subscribers**. Deferred revenue from consumers would likely only exist if they prepaid for an annual subscription in advance.
3.  **Lack of Segmentation:** The disclosures do **not** break down the total consolidated deferred revenue balance into its Space vs. Connectivity components, nor do they separate Connectivity's deferred revenue into enterprise vs. consumer categories.

**Conclusion on Deferred Revenue Composition:** We know that **Connectivity enterprise and government contracts are a major contributor to the total deferred revenue balance**, but the exact amount attributable to the Connectivity segment is not disclosed. The standard consumer broadband model does not appear to generate significant deferred revenue.

### Summary Answer

| Question | Disclosure Found? | Details from Provided Excerpts |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Separate Hardware Revenue for Connectivity?** | **No.** | Starlink Kit sales are a distinct performance obligation, but revenue is reported combined with service revenue in the single Connectivity segment figure. No quantitative split is provided. |
| **Deferred Revenue Composition for Connectivity?** | **Partially.** | It is stated that **Connectivity enterprise and government contracts are a primary source** of the company's total deferred revenue. However, the specific balance for the Connectivity segment is not broken out from the consolidated total. |

To find a precise split, you would need to look for non-GAAP supplements, management commentary in earnings calls, or potentially a more detailed revenue breakdown in the full annual report's segment footnote (though the excerpts provided suggest this level of detail is not included there either).

# Starlink Mobile: MNO Partnerships, Revenue Model & Metrics

## MNO Partners

The filing states SpaceX has partnerships with **approximately 30 MNOs across six continents**, covering an area home to approximately **1.9 billion people**. Named partners include:

- **T-Mobile** (United States)
- One NZ (New Zealand)
- Optus (Australia)
- Telstra (Australia)
- Rogers (Canada)
- KDDI (Japan)
- Salt (Switzerland)
- Entel (Chile)
- Kyivstar (Ukraine)
- VMO2 (UK)

The filing also notes that "Starlink Mobile is already commercially available for messaging in select markets."

---

## Revenue Model

The filing describes a **dual fee structure** with MNOs:

> "We charge MNOs either a **fixed fee** or a **per-mobile user fee-based amount**, which is typically passed through to the customer via the carrier."

Additionally, the filing references a second revenue mechanism:

> "We also earn Starlink Mobile revenue through **revenue-sharing arrangements** with MNO partners, based on connectivity services included in their plans."

Key structural details:
- Starlink Mobile agreements are **multi-year contractual obligations** ranging from **one to five years**, though customers can generally terminate at any time
- Starlink Mobile services have **one performance obligation** (unlike broadband, which typically bundles a kit + service)
- Starlink Mobile revenue is categorized under the **Enterprise & Government** sub-segment of the Connectivity segment
- Revenue from advanced payments is recognized **ratably over the subscription term or based on actual data consumption**

---

## Quantification of Users

The most concrete user metric provided:

> "As of **March 31, 2026**, we provide Starlink Mobile services to approximately **7.4 million monthly unique devices** across approximately 30 countries."

---

## What's NOT Broken Out

The filing does **not** provide a standalone Starlink Mobile revenue figure. Starlink Mobile revenue is embedded within the Enterprise & Government sub-segment of the Connectivity segment. The filing states:

- In 2025, **consumer subscribers represented over 60%** of Connectivity segment revenue
- Starlink Mobile is expected to become a **"significant new contributor"** of Connectivity segment growth

There is no disclosure of the exact revenue Starlink Mobile generated, nor its percentage of Connectivity segment revenue.

---

## Market Sizing Context

The filing does provide a top-down market estimate: using region-specific ARPU assumptions (weighted average of **$8/month per user** globally), and the total number of connected devices globally, SpaceX estimates the **Starlink Mobile market opportunity at $740 billion**.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, are there any additional operational metrics for the Enterprise & Government portion of Connectivity, such as number of enterprise customers, number of Starshield contracts, maritime vessels served, aviation customers, or any other quantitative measures that would help model the Enterprise & Government revenue stream? Also look for any discussion of pricing or contract sizes for enterprise/government services.

results.content:
# Enterprise & Government Operational Metrics Review

Based on a thorough review of all the document chunks you provided, **no dedicated operational metrics are disclosed for the Enterprise & Government portion of the Connectivity segment**. The document does not provide:

- ❌ Number of enterprise customers
- ❌ Number of Starshield contracts
- ❌ Number of maritime vessels served
- ❌ Number of aviation aircraft installed
- ❌ Specific contract sizes (other than the retention threshold)
- ❌ Enterprise-specific ARPU

---

## What IS Available

The following quantitative and qualitative data points can be extracted to help model Enterprise & Government revenue:

### Revenue Figures
| Period | Enterprise & Government Revenue |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | $1,109 million |
| Q1 2025 | $983 million |
| Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025 change | +$126 million (+12.8%) |

**Full-year Connectivity segment Enterprise & Government revenue drivers:**

- **2025 vs 2024:** +$1,411 million from enterprise and government customers — enterprise connectivity +$1,218M (including mobile connectivity +$632M) and government connectivity +$193M
- **2024 vs 2023:** +$1,717 million — enterprise connectivity +$466M and government connectivity +$1,250M

**Q1 2026 decomposition:** Aviation, maritime, and other enterprise +$209M; mobile connectivity +$85M; **government connectivity declined** $175M (notably absent from Q1 2025 comparison commentary)

### Retention Metric
> Since 2023, **no Starlink Enterprise customer having contributed more than $750,000 of annual revenue has voluntarily discontinued their service**.

This is the only quantified retention data point — it implies there is a meaningful cohort of enterprise accounts billing above $750K/year, but no count is given.

### Revenue Mix Context
- Consumer subscribers represented **over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue** in 2025
- Enterprise & Government is therefore **under 40%**, but growing faster in absolute dollars in recent years

### Named Enterprise Customers (by vertical)

| Vertical | Named Partners |
|---|---|
| **Aviation** | United Airlines, Southwest Airlines, Qatar Airways, Lufthansa Group, British Airways, Alaska Airlines, Hawaiian Airlines |
| **Maritime** | Carnival Corporation, Royal Caribbean Group, MSC Cruises, Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings, Maersk |
| **Land Mobility** | John Deere, California Fire Department, Brightline (Florida), Italo Treno |
| **Government** | FEMA, NOAA, Government of the Philippines, Government of Jamaica, Government of Ecuador |
| **Other** | Retail and financial services (no names) |

Many airlines have "implemented or committed to fleet-wide Starlink installations." Cruise operators have "full-fleet deployments… across thousands of vessels worldwide." These are qualitative claims — no vessel/aircraft counts.

### Maritime Addressable Market (from market overview section)
- ~99,000 commercial merchant ships (≥100 gross tons)
- ~21,000 fishing vessels
- ~4,000 cruise ships and private yachts

### Pricing & Contract Structure
- **Enterprise contracts:** Combination of subscriptions, data consumption, capacity, or other pricing models depending on customer needs
- **Starshield contracts:** Long-term, multi-year; revenue recognized over time using the **cost-to-cost input method**
- **Starlink Mobile (to MNOs):** Either a **fixed fee** or a **per-mobile user fee-based amount**, typically passed through to the end customer via the carrier
- **Deferred revenue:** Primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts — $10,179M as of Dec 31, 2024; $12,116M as of Dec 31, 2025 (a meaningful portion of which is tied to Enterprise & Government backlog)

### Starshield Specifics
- Described as a "secure, dedicated satellite network designed specifically for United States Government customers and national security applications"
- Uses "proven performance in mission-critical environments"
- Revenue recognized over time (percentage-of-completion / cost-to-cost method) for multi-year contracts
- No contract count, contract value, backlog, or customer count disclosed

---

## Key Gaps for Modeling

To build a robust Enterprise & Government revenue model, you would need externally sourced or assumed inputs for:

1. **Number of enterprise/government customers** — not disclosed
2. **Average contract value or ARPU** — not disclosed (only the $750K retention floor)
3. **Vessel/aircraft penetration rates** — not disclosed (the maritime TAM is given but not how many are served)
4. **Starshield backlog or contract pipeline** — not disclosed
5. **Enterprise vs. Government revenue split** — partially disclosed in MD&A commentary (enterprise grew faster in 2025; government declined in Q1 2026) but no exact split
6. **Starlink Mobile contribution** — included in Enterprise & Government per the footnote, and mobile connectivity revenue growth ($632M in 2025, $85M in Q1 2026) is disclosed, but no standalone revenue figure

In summary, the prospectus provides rich **named customer** and **market size** data alongside **segment-level revenue trends**, but deliberately avoids disclosing subscriber/customer counts, contract sizes, or unit-level metrics for the Enterprise & Government subsegment. The modeling approach would need to rely on the revenue trend data, the disclosed growth rates by sub-component (enterprise vs. government vs. mobile), the retention benchmark, and externally sourced assumptions about penetration rates and pricing.

arguments.final_result:
# Connectivity Revenue: Inferred Streams, Unquantified Components, and Modeling Limitations

## I. Disclosure Structure Overview

The SpaceX S-1 discloses Connectivity segment revenue at **two tiers**:

| Tier | Detail Level | Example (FY2025) |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Tier 1 — Segment** | Connectivity as a whole | $11,387M |
| **Tier 2 — Sub-categories** | Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government | Consumer: $7,208M; Enterprise & Gov: $4,179M |

No further quantitative disaggregation is provided within either sub-category.

---

## II. Revenue Streams That Can Be Reasonably Inferred

### A. Consumer Broadband (Subscription + Kit Sales)
- **Quantified at the sub-category level:** $7,208M (2025), $4,830M (2024), $2,817M (2023)
- **Drives ~63% of Connectivity revenue** (the filing states consumer subscribers represented "over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue" in 2024; the 2025 ratio is $7,208M / $11,387M = 63.3%)
- **Quantifiable operating metric:** ARPU (defined as service revenue from Starlink Subscribers divided by average subscribers and months) declined from $99/mo (2023) → $91/mo (2024) → $81/mo (2025) → $66/mo (Q1 2026)
- **Quantifiable operating metric:** Starlink Subscribers grew from 2.3M (2023) → 4.4M (2024) → 8.9M (2025) → 10.3M (Q1 2026)
- **Critical limitation on ARPU:** ARPU explicitly **excludes** managed enterprise and government customers — it only covers self-service consumer and business accounts managed through Starlink.com. It is therefore a **partial proxy** for the Consumer sub-category, not a complete measure of it.

### B. Enterprise & Government (Aggregate)
- **Quantified at the sub-category level:** $4,179M (2025), $2,769M (2024), $1,052M (2023)
- **Growth decomposition is partially disclosed in MD&A commentary:**
  - 2025 vs. 2024: Enterprise connectivity +$1,218M (of which mobile connectivity +$632M) and government connectivity +$193M
  - 2024 vs. 2023: Enterprise connectivity +$466M and government connectivity +$1,250M
  - Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025: Aviation/maritime/other enterprise +$209M; mobile connectivity +$85M; government connectivity **declined** $175M

### C. Starlink Mobile (Partial Inference)
- **7.4 million monthly unique devices** across ~30 countries as of Q1 2026
- Mobile connectivity revenue growth of **+$632M in 2025** and **+$85M in Q1 2026** is disclosed directionally
- However, no standalone Starlink Mobile revenue figure is provided — it is embedded in the Enterprise & Government line

---

## III. Revenue Streams That Remain Unquantified

| Revenue Stream | Disclosure Status |
|---------------|-------------------|
| **Starlink Kit (hardware) revenue** | Identified as a distinct performance obligation (recognized at point in time upon delivery), but **not separately quantified** from subscription/service revenue within either the Consumer or Enterprise & Government lines. Kit costs are listed in cost of revenue, but the revenue figure is not disclosed. |
| **Subscription revenue (Consumer)** | Cannot be isolated from Kit revenue within the Consumer sub-category. The ARPU metric approximates recurring service revenue for the self-service subscriber base, but Kit sales are commingled. |
| **Starshield (government connectivity)** | Discussed extensively as a long-term contract business recognized under cost-to-cost percentage-of-completion. Revenue is **not separately quantified**. Only directional growth figures are provided (e.g., +$1,250M in 2024, -$175M in Q1 2026). Contributes to total backlog ($27.6B as of Q1 2026) but Starshield's share of backlog is not disclosed. |
| **Starlink Mobile (standalone)** | 7.4M monthly unique devices disclosed. Revenue growth increments are provided, but **no standalone revenue figure** is given. Revenue-sharing and fixed-fee/per-user fee models are described qualitatively. |
| **Enterprise connectivity by vertical** | Aviation, maritime, and land mobility are discussed with named customers (United Airlines, Carnival, John Deere, etc.) but **no revenue, customer count, or per-unit pricing** is disclosed for any vertical. |
| **Enterprise vs. Government split** | MD&A commentary provides directional growth figures (e.g., enterprise grew faster in 2025; government declined in Q1 2026), but **no exact revenue split** between enterprise and government is provided for any period. |
| **Geographic revenue mix** | Not disclosed for Connectivity. ARPU decline is attributed to international expansion, but no regional breakdown is given. |
| **Deferred revenue by segment** | Total consolidated deferred revenue is disclosed ($12,116M at YE2025), and Connectivity enterprise/government contracts are cited as a "primary" driver, but **no segment-level deferred revenue figure** is provided. |

---

## IV. Modeling Limitations

### 1. Hardware vs. Service Revenue Commingling
The most fundamental modeling constraint is the inability to separate **recurring subscription revenue** from **one-time hardware (Starlink Kit) sales** within Connectivity. This matters because:
- Subscription revenue has higher margins, is recurring, and drives valuation multiples
- Kit revenue is non-recurring, lower-margin, and creates "lumpiness" correlated with subscriber growth phases
- The mix likely shifts over time as the installed base matures (fewer new kit purchases per subscriber added)
- Without this split, analysts cannot independently model churn-adjusted recurring revenue or hardware ASP trends

### 2. ARPU Metric Is a Partial Proxy
The disclosed ARPU metric **excludes all managed enterprise and government revenue** by definition. It only covers self-service consumer and business accounts. This means:
- ARPU decline reflects geographic mix shift and lower-priced plan adoption in the self-service base
- It does **not** reflect the pricing trajectory of aviation, maritime, Starshield, or Starlink Mobile contracts
- It cannot be used to back into total Connectivity service revenue (the numerator and denominator are deliberately narrower than the full segment)
- An analyst using ARPU × total subscribers would significantly understate segment revenue

### 3. Enterprise & Government Is a "Black Box"
Despite being $4.2B in 2025 and growing, the Enterprise & Government sub-category has almost no unit-level disclosure:
- No customer count (only a $750K retention floor benchmark)
- No per-vertical revenue (aviation, maritime, mobility, government)
- No contract size or pricing disclosure
- No subscriber/device count for enterprise (Starlink Mobile's 7.4M devices is the only unit metric, and it crosses enterprise/consumer boundaries via MNO partnerships)
- Revenue recognition complexity (Starshield uses cost-to-cost percentage-of-completion with significant management estimation) introduces earnings quality risk that cannot be externally audited without contract-level data

### 4. Starshield Revenue Recognition Complexity
Starshield's cost-to-cost method requires management estimates of total costs at completion. The auditor identified this as a **Critical Audit Matter (CAM)**. This means:
- Revenue recognized in any period is a function of estimated total contract costs, not billings or cash received
- Changes in estimates produce cumulative catch-up adjustments
- External modelers cannot independently verify the reasonableness of recognized revenue without contract-level cost data
- The Q1 2026 government revenue decline of $175M could reflect contract timing, estimate changes, or completion of existing contracts — the filing does not specify

### 5. Backlog Ambiguity
Total backlog is $27.6B (Q1 2026), with $13.2B in deferred revenue and the remainder as un-invoiced performance obligations. However:
- Backlog spans **both** Space and Connectivity segments — the split is not disclosed
- Within Connectivity, the enterprise vs. government vs. mobile share of backlog is unknown
- The 36%/46%/18% recognition timeline (1 year / 1-3 years / 3+ years) applies to consolidated backlog, not Connectivity-specific

### 6. Starlink Mobile Revenue Model Opacity
Starlink Mobile uses both fixed-fee and per-user fee structures with revenue sharing. With 7.4M monthly unique devices:
- The effective revenue per device is unknowable without the standalone revenue figure
- The mix of MNOs using fixed vs. per-user pricing is not disclosed
- Revenue-sharing percentages vary by partner and are not disclosed
- The TAM estimate ($740B based on $8/mo ARPU globally) suggests enormous upside but provides no basis for near-term revenue modeling

### 7. Retention Metrics Are Minimal
The sole retention disclosure — no enterprise customer contributing >$750K/year has voluntarily discontinued since 2023 — provides:
- A floor for meaningful enterprise accounts, but no count of such accounts
- No cohort analysis or net revenue retention rate
- No churn metric for consumer subscribers

---

## V. Summary

**What CAN be modeled with filing data:**
- Total Connectivity segment revenue (trend and growth rate)
- Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government revenue (aggregate level)
- Directional Enterprise & Government decomposition (enterprise vs. government vs. mobile growth increments in MD&A commentary)
- Subscriber count trajectory and ARPU trajectory for the self-service base
- Segment profitability (income from operations, Adjusted EBITDA)

**What CANNOT be modeled from filing data alone:**
- Hardware vs. service revenue split within any sub-category
- Standalone Starshield, Starlink Mobile, or vertical-specific (aviation, maritime, mobility) revenue
- Enterprise vs. government exact revenue split
- Geographic revenue distribution
- Effective pricing per enterprise contract or per Starlink Mobile device
- Segment-level deferred revenue or backlog allocation

The disclosure structure is deliberately designed to showcase aggregate growth metrics and segment profitability while protecting competitive-sensitive contract-level economics. This creates significant analytical uncertainty around the composition, quality, and sustainability of the revenue mix within Connectivity.

# Connectivity Revenue: Inferred Streams, Unquantified Components, and Modeling Limitations

## I. Disclosure Structure Overview

The SpaceX S-1 discloses Connectivity segment revenue at **two tiers**:

| Tier | Detail Level | Example (FY2025) |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Tier 1 — Segment** | Connectivity as a whole | $11,387M |
| **Tier 2 — Sub-categories** | Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government | Consumer: $7,208M; Enterprise & Gov: $4,179M |

No further quantitative disaggregation is provided within either sub-category.

---

## II. Revenue Streams That Can Be Reasonably Inferred

### A. Consumer Broadband (Subscription + Kit Sales)
- **Quantified at the sub-category level:** $7,208M (2025), $4,830M (2024), $2,817M (2023)
- **Drives ~63% of Connectivity revenue** (the filing states consumer subscribers represented "over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue" in 2024; the 2025 ratio is $7,208M / $11,387M = 63.3%)
- **Quantifiable operating metric:** ARPU (defined as service revenue from Starlink Subscribers divided by average subscribers and months) declined from $99/mo (2023) → $91/mo (2024) → $81/mo (2025) → $66/mo (Q1 2026)
- **Quantifiable operating metric:** Starlink Subscribers grew from 2.3M (2023) → 4.4M (2024) → 8.9M (2025) → 10.3M (Q1 2026)
- **Critical limitation on ARPU:** ARPU explicitly **excludes** managed enterprise and government customers — it only covers self-service consumer and business accounts managed through Starlink.com. It is therefore a **partial proxy** for the Consumer sub-category, not a complete measure of it.

### B. Enterprise & Government (Aggregate)
- **Quantified at the sub-category level:** $4,179M (2025), $2,769M (2024), $1,052M (2023)
- **Growth decomposition is partially disclosed in MD&A commentary:**
  - 2025 vs. 2024: Enterprise connectivity +$1,218M (of which mobile connectivity +$632M) and government connectivity +$193M
  - 2024 vs. 2023: Enterprise connectivity +$466M and government connectivity +$1,250M
  - Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025: Aviation/maritime/other enterprise +$209M; mobile connectivity +$85M; government connectivity **declined** $175M

### C. Starlink Mobile (Partial Inference)
- **7.4 million monthly unique devices** across ~30 countries as of Q1 2026
- Mobile connectivity revenue growth of **+$632M in 2025** and **+$85M in Q1 2026** is disclosed directionally
- However, no standalone Starlink Mobile revenue figure is provided — it is embedded in the Enterprise & Government line

---

## III. Revenue Streams That Remain Unquantified

| Revenue Stream | Disclosure Status |
|---------------|-------------------|
| **Starlink Kit (hardware) revenue** | Identified as a distinct performance obligation (recognized at point in time upon delivery), but **not separately quantified** from subscription/service revenue within either the Consumer or Enterprise & Government lines. Kit costs are listed in cost of revenue, but the revenue figure is not disclosed. |
| **Subscription revenue (Consumer)** | Cannot be isolated from Kit revenue within the Consumer sub-category. The ARPU metric approximates recurring service revenue for the self-service subscriber base, but Kit sales are commingled. |
| **Starshield (government connectivity)** | Discussed extensively as a long-term contract business recognized under cost-to-cost percentage-of-completion. Revenue is **not separately quantified**. Only directional growth figures are provided (e.g., +$1,250M in 2024, -$175M in Q1 2026). Contributes to total backlog ($27.6B as of Q1 2026) but Starshield's share of backlog is not disclosed. |
| **Starlink Mobile (standalone)** | 7.4M monthly unique devices disclosed. Revenue growth increments are provided, but **no standalone revenue figure** is given. Revenue-sharing and fixed-fee/per-user fee models are described qualitatively. |
| **Enterprise connectivity by vertical** | Aviation, maritime, and land mobility are discussed with named customers (United Airlines, Carnival, John Deere, etc.) but **no revenue, customer count, or per-unit pricing** is disclosed for any vertical. |
| **Enterprise vs. Government split** | MD&A commentary provides directional growth figures (e.g., enterprise grew faster in 2025; government declined in Q1 2026), but **no exact revenue split** between enterprise and government is provided for any period. |
| **Geographic revenue mix** | Not disclosed for Connectivity. ARPU decline is attributed to international expansion, but no regional breakdown is given. |
| **Deferred revenue by segment** | Total consolidated deferred revenue is disclosed ($12,116M at YE2025), and Connectivity enterprise/government contracts are cited as a "primary" driver, but **no segment-level deferred revenue figure** is provided. |

---

## IV. Modeling Limitations

### 1. Hardware vs. Service Revenue Commingling
The most fundamental modeling constraint is the inability to separate **recurring subscription revenue** from **one-time hardware (Starlink Kit) sales** within Connectivity. This matters because:
- Subscription revenue has higher margins, is recurring, and drives valuation multiples
- Kit revenue is non-recurring, lower-margin, and creates "lumpiness" correlated with subscriber growth phases
- The mix likely shifts over time as the installed base matures (fewer new kit purchases per subscriber added)
- Without this split, analysts cannot independently model churn-adjusted recurring revenue or hardware ASP trends

### 2. ARPU Metric Is a Partial Proxy
The disclosed ARPU metric **excludes all managed enterprise and government revenue** by definition. It only covers self-service consumer and business accounts. This means:
- ARPU decline reflects geographic mix shift and lower-priced plan adoption in the self-service base
- It does **not** reflect the pricing trajectory of aviation, maritime, Starshield, or Starlink Mobile contracts
- It cannot be used to back into total Connectivity service revenue (the numerator and denominator are deliberately narrower than the full segment)
- An analyst using ARPU × total subscribers would significantly understate segment revenue

### 3. Enterprise & Government Is a "Black Box"
Despite being $4.2B in 2025 and growing, the Enterprise & Government sub-category has almost no unit-level disclosure:
- No customer count (only a $750K retention floor benchmark)
- No per-vertical revenue (aviation, maritime, mobility, government)
- No contract size or pricing disclosure
- No subscriber/device count for enterprise (Starlink Mobile's 7.4M devices is the only unit metric, and it crosses enterprise/consumer boundaries via MNO partnerships)
- Revenue recognition complexity (Starshield uses cost-to-cost percentage-of-completion with significant management estimation) introduces earnings quality risk that cannot be externally audited without contract-level data

### 4. Starshield Revenue Recognition Complexity
Starshield's cost-to-cost method requires management estimates of total costs at completion. The auditor identified this as a **Critical Audit Matter (CAM)**. This means:
- Revenue recognized in any period is a function of estimated total contract costs, not billings or cash received
- Changes in estimates produce cumulative catch-up adjustments
- External modelers cannot independently verify the reasonableness of recognized revenue without contract-level cost data
- The Q1 2026 government revenue decline of $175M could reflect contract timing, estimate changes, or completion of existing contracts — the filing does not specify

### 5. Backlog Ambiguity
Total backlog is $27.6B (Q1 2026), with $13.2B in deferred revenue and the remainder as un-invoiced performance obligations. However:
- Backlog spans **both** Space and Connectivity segments — the split is not disclosed
- Within Connectivity, the enterprise vs. government vs. mobile share of backlog is unknown
- The 36%/46%/18% recognition timeline (1 year / 1-3 years / 3+ years) applies to consolidated backlog, not Connectivity-specific

### 6. Starlink Mobile Revenue Model Opacity
Starlink Mobile uses both fixed-fee and per-user fee structures with revenue sharing. With 7.4M monthly unique devices:
- The effective revenue per device is unknowable without the standalone revenue figure
- The mix of MNOs using fixed vs. per-user pricing is not disclosed
- Revenue-sharing percentages vary by partner and are not disclosed
- The TAM estimate ($740B based on $8/mo ARPU globally) suggests enormous upside but provides no basis for near-term revenue modeling

### 7. Retention Metrics Are Minimal
The sole retention disclosure — no enterprise customer contributing >$750K/year has voluntarily discontinued since 2023 — provides:
- A floor for meaningful enterprise accounts, but no count of such accounts
- No cohort analysis or net revenue retention rate
- No churn metric for consumer subscribers

---

## V. Summary

**What CAN be modeled with filing data:**
- Total Connectivity segment revenue (trend and growth rate)
- Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government revenue (aggregate level)
- Directional Enterprise & Government decomposition (enterprise vs. government vs. mobile growth increments in MD&A commentary)
- Subscriber count trajectory and ARPU trajectory for the self-service base
- Segment profitability (income from operations, Adjusted EBITDA)

**What CANNOT be modeled from filing data alone:**
- Hardware vs. service revenue split within any sub-category
- Standalone Starshield, Starlink Mobile, or vertical-specific (aviation, maritime, mobility) revenue
- Enterprise vs. government exact revenue split
- Geographic revenue distribution
- Effective pricing per enterprise contract or per Starlink Mobile device
- Segment-level deferred revenue or backlog allocation

The disclosure structure is deliberately designed to showcase aggregate growth metrics and segment profitability while protecting competitive-sensitive contract-level economics. This creates significant analytical uncertainty around the composition, quality, and sustainability of the revenue mix within Connectivity.
